Ezra arrives not as a military commander or political appointee, but as a scribe—a man whose life has been shaped by the Law of the LORD. The genealogy that opens this chapter traces his descent through the priestly line all the way back to Aaron, establishing his credentials. Yet what matters most is not his ancestry but his calling: he has set his heart to study the Law, to practice it, and to teach it. This is not casual religious interest. This is the deepest orientation of his life. When God's hand rests upon someone, it equips them for a specific purpose, and Ezra's purpose is to restore God's people to obedience to His Word. The remarkable fact is that a pagan king—Artaxerxes—becomes the instrument through which this restoration happens. The king grants Ezra's requests, funds the temple, and grants him judicial authority. The reason is not the king's piety, but God's working. "The hand of the LORD his God was upon him."

What strikes us about the royal decree is how thoroughly it subordinates earthly power to heavenly authority. Artaxerxes himself acknowledges that "whatever is commanded by the God of heaven must be done diligently." He exempts God's servants from taxation and grants unlimited resources for the temple's work. This is not because the king has become an Israelite, but because he recognizes that there is a power greater than his own. The decree reveals something essential about God's sovereignty: He can move the hearts of nations. Kings and counselors serve His purposes whether they fully understand it or not. The resources flowing from Babylon to Jerusalem—gold, silver, animals for sacrifice—are not Artaxerxes' gifts, ultimately. They are God's provision for His people and His house.

Yet the chapter does not present this as magical or effortless. Ezra must decide to go. He must gather others willing to make the journey. He must receive the king's commission, accept the responsibility of judging and teaching in Judah, and bear the weight of enforcing God's Law. The chapter ends with Ezra's own prayer of thanksgiving: he acknowledges that God has put it into the king's heart to honor the temple, that God has shown him favor, and that because God's hand was upon him, he took courage. Courage—not confidence in human support or royal favor, but courage grounded in the presence of God's hand. This is what moves ordinary people to extraordinary faithfulness. As you read this account of restoration, ask yourself: Have I set my heart to study God's Word, to obey it, and to teach it? Do I recognize God's hand working through the circumstances of my life? And am I willing to take courage in His presence, even when the task before me seems too large?
